title: Database Normalization
summary: First, Second, and Third Normal Forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF), functional dependencies, and BCNF.
content_html:
What is Normalization?

Normalization is the process of organizing a database schema to reduce data redundancy and improve data integrity. It works through a series of normal forms.

Functional Dependency

Attribute B is functionally dependent on A (written A → B) if knowing A uniquely determines B.

StudentID → Name (knowing ID determines Name)
StudentID, CourseID → Grade (composite key determines Grade)

First Normal Form (1NF)

A table is in 1NF when:

  • All columns have atomic (indivisible) values.
  • No repeating groups or arrays.
  • Each row is unique (has a primary key).
VIOLATION: Student(ID, Name, Courses) where Courses = "Math, Physics"
FIX: Create separate rows for each course.

Second Normal Form (2NF)

Table is in 2NF when it is in 1NF AND every non-key attribute is fully functionally dependent on the entire primary key (no partial dependencies — only matters when PK is composite).

VIOLATION: Order(OrderID, ProductID, ProductName, Quantity)
  ProductID → ProductName (partial: ProductName depends only on ProductID, not both)
  
FIX: Split into:
  Order(OrderID, ProductID, Quantity)   -- full dependency on composite PK
  Product(ProductID, ProductName)        -- separate table

Third Normal Form (3NF)

Table is in 3NF when it is in 2NF AND there are no transitive dependencies (non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute).

VIOLATION: Employee(EmpID, DeptID, DeptName)
  EmpID → DeptID → DeptName  (transitive: DeptName depends on DeptID, not EmpID)
  
FIX: Split into:
  Employee(EmpID, DeptID)
  Department(DeptID, DeptName)

Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)

Stricter than 3NF: every determinant must be a candidate key. Handles edge cases 3NF misses when there are multiple overlapping candidate keys.

Benefits of Normalization

  • Eliminates redundancy (data stored once).
  • Prevents update anomalies (only update one place).
  • Prevents insertion/deletion anomalies.
  • Trade-off: more tables may require more JOINs (performance vs. integrity).

exercises.title: 1NF atomic values
exercises.prompt: First Normal Form requires that all column values are:

exercises.title: Partial dependency
exercises.prompt: A partial dependency occurs when a non-key attribute depends on:
options: Only part of a composite primary key
exercises.answer: Only part of a composite primary key

exercises.title: 2NF violation fix
exercises.prompt: To fix a 2NF violation (partial dependency), you should:
options: Split the table so dependent attributes move to their own table
exercises.answer: Split the table so dependent attributes move to their own table

exercises.title: Transitive dependency
options: A non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute
A key depends on a non-key
All attributes depend on the key
exercises.answer: A non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute

exercises.title: Functional dependency notation
options: A is a foreign key for B

exercises.title: Normalization benefit
exercises.prompt: A key benefit of normalization is:
options: Reduced data redundancy and fewer update anomalies
exercises.answer: Reduced data redundancy and fewer update anomalies

exercises.title: BCNF requirement
exercises.prompt: BCNF requires that every determinant must be:

exercises.title: Normalization trade-off
exercises.prompt: A downside of high normalization is:
options: More tables may require more JOINs, affecting performance
exercises.answer: More tables may require more JOINs, affecting performance

exercises.title: Update anomaly
exercises.prompt: Update anomalies in un-normalized tables occur because:
options: The same data is stored in multiple places
exercises.answer: The same data is stored in multiple places
